findings.summary: Module-level and method docstrings frequently restate what the code already makes obvious
evidence: paperless.py line 5-12: docstring restates 'This module provides a client for interacting with the Paperless-ngx API. It encapsulates all the logic for making authenticated requests...' which is self-evident from the class name and methods
concurrency.py line 52: docstring 'Context manager that acquires the LLM concurrency semaphore' restates the method name exactly
shutdown.py: 4-line functions get multi-line docstrings explaining the obvious
claims.py lines 34-45: the docstring repeats the 4-step workflow that is immediately visible in the function body below it
Nearly every function has Args/Returns sections even for trivial single-parameter functions like is_shutdown_requested() -> bool
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings to only document non-obvious behavior, edge cases, or domain reasoning. Remove Args/Returns sections where the type signature is self-documenting.

findings.summary: RST-style section headers with === underlines in module docstrings are uniformly applied, a hallmark of LLM-generated documentation
evidence: Every module docstring follows the exact same pattern: title on line 2, followed by '=====' underline, then a structured description
This uniformity across 20+ modules is unnatural for human-written code and is a signature pattern of LLM-generated code
findings.suggestion: Simplify module docstrings to a single descriptive sentence or short paragraph. Remove RST-style headers unless generating Sphinx documentation.

findings.summary: Unreachable code paths with defensive RuntimeError raises that add no value
evidence: retry.py line 98: 'raise RuntimeError("unreachable: retry loop exited without return or raise")' -- the for-loop with range() always completes; this line is truly unreachable
paperless.py lines 129-133: 'raise RuntimeError(... "no candidates tried")' with a pragma: no cover comment acknowledging it is unreachable
findings.suggestion: Remove unreachable RuntimeError guards. If type checkers need a return, use typing.assert_never or NoReturn annotations instead.

findings.summary: Step-by-step numbered comments (Step 1, Step 2, etc.) that restate code intent already clear from method names
evidence: claims.py lines 49-98: '# Step 1: Refresh', '# Step 2: Check', '# Step 3: Patch', '# Step 4: Verify' -- these comments add nothing beyond what the code and the docstring already explain
findings.suggestion: Remove numbered step comments. The code is already well-structured and self-documenting.

findings.summary: common/__init__.py is a long hand-written catalog of every module in prose, duplicating what the file listing already shows
evidence: __init__.py lines 1-21: 21-line docstring that enumerates every module with a dash-delimited description, mirroring what ls src/common/ would show
findings.suggestion: Replace with a one-line package purpose statement. Let the individual module docstrings serve as documentation.

findings.summary: OcrProvider ABC has exactly one implementation (OpenAIProvider) with no evidence of extension pressure
evidence: OcrProvider ABC defined at line 60 with a single concrete subclass OpenAIProvider at line 89
No other OcrProvider subclass exists in the codebase
The docstring says 'Subclass this to add support for a new vision-capable LLM backend' but no second backend exists
findings.suggestion: Remove the ABC and use OpenAIProvider directly. Introduce an ABC only when a second implementation materializes.

findings.summary: Module-level wrapper functions init_llm_semaphore() and llm_semaphore() merely forward to the module-level llm_limiter singleton
evidence: init_llm_semaphore() at line 67 is a one-line wrapper: llm_limiter.init(max_concurrent)
llm_semaphore() at line 76 is a one-line wrapper: with llm_limiter.acquire(): yield
Callers could use llm_limiter.init() and llm_limiter.acquire() directly
findings.suggestion: Remove the free-function wrappers and have callers use the llm_limiter singleton directly, or remove the class and keep only the free functions.

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin contains a single method (_create_completion) making it a thin forwarding layer
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin has one method: _create_completion which just calls openai.chat.completions.create(**kwargs) with retry and semaphore
Used via multiple inheritance in OpenAIProvider and ClassificationProvider
The mixin pattern adds indirection for what could be a plain function taking settings as a parameter
findings.suggestion: Replace the mixin with a standalone function create_completion(settings, **kwargs) that both providers call directly.

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses getattr with string attribute names instead of direct attribute access
evidence: Line 72-74: getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr) and getattr(settings, pre_tag_id_attr) where both callers pass known literal strings
This trades compile-time attribute checking for runtime string-based lookup
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs directly as int parameters, or use a small config dataclass/NamedTuple for the two tag IDs needed.

findings.summary: Both daemons create a new PaperlessClient per document, duplicating the same pattern
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 44: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside _process_document
classifier/daemon.py line 77: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside process_document
Both follow the identical try/finally pattern with paperless.close()
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared context manager or factory function for per-document PaperlessClient lifecycle management.

findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error takes a structlog BoundLogger as a parameter instead of using its own module logger
evidence: tags.py line 185: 'log: structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger' is a required parameter
Both workers pass their module-level 'log' to this function
This creates an unusual seam where a utility function depends on caller-provided logging infrastructure rather than using its own
findings.suggestion: Have finalize_document_with_error use its own module-level logger (structlog.get_logger(__name__)) and remove the log parameter.

findings.summary: retry decorator is tightly coupled to instance methods via self.settings convention
evidence: retry.py line 76: 'settings: RetrySettings = self.settings' -- the decorator hardcodes that the first positional arg is self and it must have a .settings attribute
This prevents using @retry on free functions, classmethods, or objects that store settings differently
findings.suggestion: Accept a settings accessor (callable or the settings object itself) as a decorator parameter instead of relying on self.settings convention.

findings.summary: Error/refusal phrase lists are defined in two separate places with partially overlapping content
evidence: config.py lines 113-119: OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS default list includes 'i can't assist', 'i cannot assist', etc.
constants.py lines 62-68: ERROR_PHRASES tuple includes 'i'm sorry, i can't assist with that.', 'i can't assist with that', etc.
These two lists serve the same purpose (detecting refusals) but live in different modules with different subsets of phrases
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single canonical refusal phrase list in constants.py and have config.py reference it for defaults.

findings.summary: Multiple modules include 'Typical usage::' code examples in docstrings that restate import paths already obvious from the module structure
evidence: retry.py lines 17-27: 'Typical usage::' example showing import and decorator application
concurrency.py lines 9-16: 'Typical usage::' example
text_assembly.py lines 9-17: 'Typical usage::' example
These are LLM-hallmark patterns providing usage examples for internal modules that have exactly one or two call sites
findings.suggestion: Remove 'Typical usage' blocks from internal modules. They are only useful for public library APIs.
